questions.question_text: A seller lists her home with Broker A under an exclusive right to sell agreement. The seller finds the buyer herself without any help from the broker. Does Broker A earn a commission?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the broker earns regardless of who finds the buyer
distractors: No, the broker only earns when directly involved
Only if the broker can prove marketing efforts
Only half the agreed commission rate
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive right to sell listing, the broker earns the commission regardless of who procures the buyer, even if the seller finds the buyer independently. This is the most common listing type in Georgia and provides the strongest protection for the broker.

questions.question_text: A seller signs an exclusive agency listing with Broker B. The seller later finds a buyer through a personal contact and closes the sale. Must the seller pay Broker B?
questions.correct_answer: No, the seller is not obligated to pay under exclusive agency
distractors: Yes, any exclusive listing guarantees the broker's commission
Yes, unless the seller gave 30 days written notice
Only if Broker B incurred marketing expenses
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, the broker earns a commission only if the broker or another cooperating broker finds the buyer. If the seller independently locates the buyer, no commission is owed. This distinguishes exclusive agency from exclusive right to sell.

questions.question_text: Under BRRETA, what is required for a listing agreement to be enforceable in Georgia?
questions.correct_answer: The listing must be in writing
distractors: The listing must be filed with the local MLS
The listing must be notarized by a public notary
The listing must be approved by GREC in advance
questions.explanation: BRRETA defines a brokerage engagement as a written contract — without one, the broker works with the party as a customer rather than a client. GREC Rule 520-1-.06 additionally requires every exclusive brokerage agreement to fully set forth its terms and carry a definite expiration date, with a copy furnished to each signer. MLS filing, notarization, and GREC pre-approval are never required.

questions.question_text: A property owner gives open listings to three different brokerages simultaneously. Which broker earns the commission when the property sells?
questions.correct_answer: Only the broker who was the procuring cause
distractors: All three brokers split the commission equally
The broker who received the listing first
The broker with the longest active listing period
questions.explanation: In an open listing, only the broker who is the procuring cause of the sale earns the commission. Multiple brokers may hold open listings on the same property, but only the one who actually brings the buyer earns compensation.

questions.question_text: Which listing type provides the MOST protection for a Georgia broker's commission under all sale scenarios?
distractors: Exclusive agency listing
Open listing agreement
Net listing arrangement
questions.explanation: The exclusive right to sell provides the most commission protection because the broker earns regardless of who procures the buyer. Under exclusive agency, the seller can avoid paying if they find the buyer. Open listings pay only the procuring broker.

questions.question_text: What type of license must a qualifying broker hold to operate a real estate firm in Georgia?
distractors: An associate broker license with five years experience
A community association manager license
A salesperson license with qualifying endorsement
questions.explanation: Every real estate firm in Georgia must have a qualifying broker who holds an active broker license. An associate broker or salesperson license is insufficient. The qualifying broker is responsible for all firm operations and licensee supervision.

questions.question_text: A Georgia brokerage opens a second office location in another city. What does Georgia license law require for this additional location?
questions.correct_answer: Compliance with local business ordinances — GREC does not register branch offices
distractors: Registration of the branch office with GREC
A separate broker license for each location
A second qualifying broker for the branch office
questions.explanation: Georgia license law imposes no GREC branch-office registration or licensing. O.C.G.A. 43-40-18(a) requires only that licensees operating from more than one place of business comply with all local business ordinances. One firm license and one qualifying broker cover all of a firm's locations, and the qualifying broker remains responsible for supervision at each.

questions.question_text: A salesperson in a Georgia firm fails to properly document a transaction. Who bears regulatory responsibility for this oversight?
questions.correct_answer: The qualifying broker of the firm
distractors: Only the salesperson who handled the transaction
The buyer's attorney who reviewed the documents
The closing company that processed the sale
questions.explanation: The qualifying broker is responsible for all affiliated licensees' actions, including proper documentation. While the salesperson may also face discipline, the broker bears ultimate regulatory responsibility for supervision and transaction file maintenance.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker in Georgia runs the firm entirely from a home office with no commercial signage. Does this meet Georgia license law requirements?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the home address is on file with GREC as the firm's business address
distractors: No, Georgia requires a commercial office that is open to the public
No, Georgia requires an office sign identifying the brokerage
Only if the broker supervises fewer than three licensees
questions.explanation: Current Georgia license law contains no definite-place-of-business, office, or signage requirement. A firm must have a business address on file with GREC and must notify the commission in writing within 30 days of any address change (O.C.G.A. 43-40-19(a)); brokers operating from any location must also comply with local business ordinances (43-40-18(a)). A home-based brokerage is fully permissible.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a responsibility of the qualifying broker regarding trust accounts?
questions.correct_answer: Managing and overseeing all trust account activity
distractors: Delegating trust account authority to any licensee
Allowing salespersons to make independent disbursements
Reporting trust account balances to clients monthly
questions.explanation: The qualifying broker is ultimately responsible for trust account management, including deposits, disbursements, and reconciliation. While the broker may designate a specific person to handle daily operations, the broker retains oversight responsibility.

questions.question_text: Two competing brokerages in the same market agree to charge identical commission rates. What legal issue does this raise?
questions.correct_answer: An antitrust violation through price fixing
distractors: A BRRETA violation for undisclosed agency fees
A GREC licensing regulation infraction
A fair housing violation based on market restriction
questions.explanation: Commissions in Georgia are always negotiable. When competing brokerages agree to charge the same rate, it constitutes price fixing, which is a serious antitrust violation under federal law. Each brokerage must independently set its own fees.

questions.question_text: A grateful seller writes a $2,000 bonus check directly to the salesperson who listed and sold her home. Can the salesperson accept this payment?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation must come through the salesperson's broker
distractors: Yes, bonuses from clients are always permitted
Yes, if the salesperson reports it as income
Only with written approval from both parties
questions.explanation: Under O.C.G.A. 43-40-25(b)(8), a licensee commits an unfair trade practice by accepting a commission or other valuable consideration from anyone other than the broker holding the licensee's license without that broker's consent. The proper course is for the bonus to be paid to the brokerage and passed to the salesperson through the broker. Accepting the seller's check directly, as presented here, violates the statute.

questions.question_text: A listing broker in Georgia wants to share a portion of the commission with a cooperating buyer's broker from another firm. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, brokers may share commissions with cooperating brokers
distractors: No, commissions cannot be split between firms
Only if both firms are members of the same MLS
Only with prior written approval from GREC
questions.explanation: Georgia brokers may share commissions with cooperating brokers from other firms. This is standard practice and facilitates cooperation between listing and buyer's agents. The key rule is that salespersons receive compensation only through their own broker.

questions.question_text: Under a typical Georgia listing agreement, when does the broker's commission become payable?
distractors: When the listing agreement is signed
When the buyer's offer is accepted
When the earnest money is deposited
questions.explanation: Georgia listing agreements typically make the commission payable at closing, and O.C.G.A. 43-40-20(e) bars a broker from claiming trust funds as commission until the transaction is consummated or terminated. Note the distinction: absent contrary contract terms, O.C.G.A. 10-6-32 says a commission is 'earned' when the broker procures a ready, able, and willing buyer who offers on the seller's terms — but payment is conventionally due at closing.

questions.question_text: An associate broker changes firms mid-transaction. From whom should the associate broker receive compensation for the pending sale?
questions.correct_answer: The broker the associate was with during the sale
distractors: The new broker at the associate's current firm
Directly from the closing attorney's office
The client who originally engaged the associate
questions.explanation: Compensation flows through the broker with whom the licensee was affiliated when the work was performed. Even after changing firms, the associate broker receives payment through the original broker, not the new firm or directly from any party.
